coredata.dc:title: Effects of the coupling between electrode and GQD-anthoxanthin nanocomposites for dye-sensitized solar cell: DFT and TD-DFT investigations
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Feng
Yang, Chuan-Lu
Jiang, Gang
coredata.dc:description: The photosensitizer is the key factor in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC). The design and selection of dyes photosensitizer with excellent photoelectric conversion efficiency properties are an effective way of improving the functioning of DSSC. The feasibility of the hybrid nanocomposites of graphene quantum dot (GQD) and a series of anthoxanthin dyes as the efficient photosensitizer is investigated by employing the first-principles density functional theory (DFT) and the time-dependent DFT(TDDFT). The electronic properties, absorption spectra, photoelectric conversion, and intramolecular electron transfer of the GQD-anthoxanthin nanocomposites are calculated and analyzed. The effect of the coupling between nanocomposites and the TiO2 electrode is examined to evaluate the performance of the nanocomposites. The results demonstrate the better overall performance is attributed to GQD-A and GQD-B nanocomposites because they have good charge separation state, suitable energy levels, red-shift, and absorption enhancement in the visible light range, high light-capture efficiency, and larger driving force. These findings are helpful for designing and screening novel nanocomposite photosensitizers for DSSC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photosensitizer
Nanocomposites
Optical absorption
Photoelectric conversion
Intramolecular electron transfer

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Geometrical structures and vibrational spectra of the nanocomposites
Feasibility of the nanocomposites as photosensitizers of DSSC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The molecular structures for GQD(C54H18), and anthoxanthin dyes.
The optimized geometrical structures for nanocomposites and nanocomposites/TiO2 complex systems.
The vibrational spectra for the nanocomposites, calculated by B3LYP/6-31 G(d,p)/PCM-THF.
The vibrational spectra for nanocomposites/TiO2 complex system, calculated by B3LYP/PCM-THF.
Energy levels and contour plots of the HOMO and LUMO for nanocomposites, calculated by B3LYP/6-31 G(d,p)/PCM-THF, TiO2 conduction band and redox couple of I−/I3 − electrolyte.

Energy levels and contour plots of the HOMO and LUMO for nanocomposites/TiO2 complex system, calculated by B3LYP/PCM-THF together with the energy levels of the conduction band of TiO2 and I−/I3 − represents the electrolyte.
HOMO and LUMO of nanocomposites and nanocomposites/TiO2 complex system, calculated at the B3LYP/6-31 G(d,p)/PCM-THF.

(a) The absorption spectra of anthoxanthin dyes, GQD-anthoxanthin nanocomposites, and TiO2-nanocomposites complex systems, calculated by PCM-TD-B3LYP/6-31 G(d,p) level of theory in THF. (b) The absorption spectra of anthoxanthin dyes, GQD-anthoxanthin nanocomposites, and TiO2-nanocomposites complex systems, calculated by PCM-TD-B3LYP/6-31 G(d,p) level of theory in THF.

Computed main electron transitions, maximum absorption wavelengths (λ max), oscillator strengths (f), and excitation energies (E) of the anthoxanthin dyes and nanocomposites in THF by TD-PCM-B3LYP/6-31 G(d,p).

Computed
E ox
dye *
, ΔG injec,
Δ
G dye regen
, LHE, and V oc of the anthoxanthin dyes and nanocomposites in THF by TD-PCM-B3LYP/6-31 G(d,p).

head.ce:title: Effects of the coupling between electrode and GQD-anthoxanthin nanocomposites for dye-sensitized solar cell: DFT and TD-DFT investigations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Ludong University, Yantai, 264025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 1School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Atomic and Molecular Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 2Institute of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of High Energy Density Physics and Technology, Ministry of Education, Chengdu, 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of High Energy Density Physics and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 3Key Laboratory of High Energy Density Physics and Technology, Ministry of Education, Chengdu

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photosensitizer is the key factor in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC). The design and selection of dyes photosensitizer with excellent photoelectric conversion efficiency properties are an effective way of improving the functioning of DSSC. The feasibility of the hybrid nanocomposites of graphene quantum dot (GQD) and a series of anthoxanthin dyes as the efficient photosensitizer is investigated by employing the first-principles density functional theory (DFT) and the time-dependent DFT(TDDFT). The electronic properties, absorption spectra, photoelectric conversion, and intramolecular electron transfer of the GQD-anthoxanthin nanocomposites are calculated and analyzed. The effect of the coupling between nanocomposites and the TiO2 electrode is examined to evaluate the performance of the nanocomposites. The results demonstrate the better overall performance is attributed to GQD-A and GQD-B nanocomposites because they have good charge separation state, suitable energy levels, red-shift, and absorption enhancement in the visible light range, high light-capture efficiency, and larger driving force. These findings are helpful for designing and screening novel nanocomposite photosensitizers for DSSC.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy is the most universal and primary demand for survival and continuity by nature and human beings. The energy demand is huge today due to the development of industrialization and population growth, so that there is an immense burden on conventional energy resources. Thus, it is necessary to search for renewable and environmentally friendly new energy sources to overcome these problems. The solar energy is a clean, sustainable, environmentally friendly, and the world’s most abundant energy, which is a very attractive one as an alternate source of conventional fossil fuels through solar cells. There are many kinds of solar cells, among which the dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) is certainly an excellent device that has attracted considerable attention since the report by Grätzel and co-workers in 1991 [1–4]. Due to the advantages of the high theoretical conversion efficiency, small dependence on temperature and incident light angle, high transparency, high conversion rate under low light flux, rich and non-toxic raw materials and large-scale production, DSSC has gradually become an important branch of the solar photovoltaic power generation industry, and has achieved more than 13 % photoelectric conversion efficiency [1,4]. The typical structural design of DSSC devices is composed of wide bandgap semiconductor metal oxide as photoanode, the layer of organic dyes as a photosensitizer, an electrolyte containing redox couple, and a counter electrode [5–7]. Among these components, the photoanode is a porous nanocrystalline film composed of a wide bandgap metal-oxide compounds such as TiO2, ZnO, or SnO2 while the electrolyte is usually the iodide/tri-iodide (I−/I3 -) redox couple in some organic solvent, the counter electrode must have a low charge transfer resistance, high exchange current densities for the reduction of the oxidized form of the electrolyte and the photosensitizer is the key photoelectric conversion component for capturing solar energy, generating and transmitting electrons.

To constantly improve the photovoltaic performance of DSSC, scientists have made great efforts, such as exploiting a novel photoanode [8–10], searching for new types of the counter electrode [11,12], designing and synthesizing new photosensitizer [13] and electrolytes [14,15]. However, the design of a photosensitizer is regarded as one of the most effective ways to enhance the photovoltaic performance of DSSC. Recently, organic dyes such as coumarin, indoline, triarylamine, perylene, hemicyanin, phthalocyanine, cyanine, squaraine dyes, and xanthenes are considered as the photosensitizer of DSSC [16–29], since they are very cheap and can be prepared easily as compared to other organic dyes. Nevertheless, these organic dyes often demonstrate poor photovoltaic performance because of weak binding energy with TiO2 film and low light absorption in the visible range. Moreover, the application of naturally occurring pigments such as cyanine, carotenoids, anthoxanthins, and chlorophylls for the photosensitizer of DSSC has aroused widespread-concern [30]. Earlier, researchers have used red Sicilian orange juice, rosella, Bengal rose and coumarin as a photosensitizer in DSSC [31–34]. The use of native plants in DSSC, including B. Vulgaris, Cytisus, Alcea rosea, Roselle flowers, okra plant sap, berry, red cabbage, and achiote seeds are also reported [35–37]. The ready availability, environmentally friendly, easy extraction procedures, and minimal need for further purification make these dyes become the potential photosensitizer for DSSC. In recent years, Kabir et al. [38] extracted dyes materials from red and green spinach and used in DSSC with the efficiency of 0.46 % and 0.53 %, respectively. Ishwari et al. [39] extracted the dye from Cassia fistula flowers and used it in DSSC with an efficiency of 0.21 %. Numerous efforts have been paid extensively and reports have been made by several research groups to describe and improve the properties of these natural dyes as a photosensitizer in DSSC [13,40,41]. For example, Megala et al. have reported a series of anthoxanthins belonging to the Flavones and Flavonols families that are carried out to identify the dyes with the optimum properties as a photosensitizer. They construct a series of anthoxanthin dyes including Flavones and Flavonols based on the substitution of the hydroxyl group on the B ring [13].

Despite anthoxanthin dyes could be the potential photosensitizer of DSSC, all the structures have no strong absorption in the visible light region, resulting in their low photoelectric conversion efficiency. The efficient photosensitizer should have enhanced absorption in the range from visible to infrared wavelength of the sunlight because most energy of the sunlight is included in the light region. To make naturally occurring pigments become more effective photosensitizers, one could enhance the optical absorption of these structures in the visible light range by modifying the structures with some functional ligands or hybridizing quantum dots (QDs). In fact, the photosensitizer consisting of many nanocomposites has been proved to be an efficient photoelectric conversion material, because the nanocomposites have better photovoltaic performance than the single components [42,43]. The nanocomposites are particularly suitable for the photosensitizer in DSSC because of the efficient photoelectron transfer from one component to another in the nanocomposite. Among the various QDs, graphene quantum dot (GQD) demonstrates very large charge mobility, mechanical properties combined with excellent electrical and optical properties, which make it a potential candidate for the fabrication of nanocomposites with favorable photovoltaics properties [44]. Unlike the bulk graphene, GQD has a tunable bandgap and large optical absorptivity [45,46], and exhibit strong photoluminescence (PL) [47–49]. A desirable bandgap is necessary for good photoelectronic performance. Thus, researchers often tune the band gaps of GQDs in a wide range by varying the sizes [50–52]. In addition, GQD is composed of abundant simple elements on the earth, which is environmentally friendly. In our previous studies, it has been verified that the GQD hybrid can effectively improve the light absorption properties of dye molecules [53,54]. In this work, we constructed the nanocomposites of GQD and anthoxanthin dyes to explore better photosensitizers for DSSC.

To fully evaluate the possibility of the designed nanocomposites as photosensitizer of DSSC, we optimize their geometrical structures with the hybrid density functional theory (DFT) methods and confirm the energy stability with frequency analysis. Furthermore, the coupling interaction between nanocomposites and TiO2 electrodes is examined to explore the effect of the newly designed photosensitizer. The mechanism of electron transfer, energy level, the charge spatial separation, and electronic density of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of the nanocomposites are investigated and analyzed. The aim is to screen out the suitable ones as photosensitizers of DSSC. The optical absorptions are calculated with the time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) method. The results demonstrated that the optical absorption of the present nanocomposites is obviously broadened in comparison to those of the separate anthoxanthin dyes. The key parameters light-harvesting efficiency (LHE), free energy change (ΔG inject) for electron injection, the short-circuit current density (J sc), and the open-circuit photovoltage (V oc) is also evaluated. These findings can provide a helpful guide for developing efficient photosensitizer of DSSC with the nanocomposites of GQD with anthoxanthin dyes.

The anthoxanthin dyes include Flavones (Flavone: 2-phenylchromen-4-one, Chrysin: 5,7-Dihydroxy-2-phenyl-4H-chromen-4-one, Apigenin: 5,7-Dihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-4H-1-benzopyran-4-one, Luteolin: 2-(3,4-Dihydroxyphenyl)-3−5,7-dihydroxy-4-chromenone) and Flavonols (3-Hydroxyflavone: 3-hydroxy-2-phenylchromen-4-one, Galangin: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-phenylchromen-4-one, Kaempferol: 3,5,7-Trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-4H-chromen-4-one, Quercetin: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxy-4H-chromen-4-one) [13]. The molecular structures of the designed anthoxanthin dyes and GQD(C54H18) is shown in Fig. 1 . To simplify, we named Flavone, Chrysin, Apigenin, Luteolin, 3-Hydroxyflavone, Galangin, Kaempferol, and Quercetin as A, B, C, D, E, F, G, and H, respectively. The present number/size(C54H18) of the cir-coronene structure of GQD is chosen according to the detailed discussion of the structural parameters and optical properties by Li et al. [55]. The geometrical molecular structures of the nanocomposites are constructed by attaching anthoxanthin dyes to GQD through an amide linkage. The optimized geometrical structures of all the nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 2 . To further explore the effect of coupling interaction between nanocomposites and TiO2 electrodes, the nanocomposites connection (TiO2)15 cluster complex system is studied, to reveal the electron injection mechanisms from the nanocomposites dyes to the TiO2 (101) surface [56,57]. The optimized geometrical structures of all the nanocomposites/TiO2 complex systems are presented in Fig. 2. The geometrical configurations and natural population analysis of the separate anthoxanthin dyes and GQD, nanocomposites, and nanocomposites/TiO2 complex systems are optimized by using the hybrid DFT method as implemented in the Gaussian 16 program [58]. The polarizable continuum model (PCM) is used to model the solvent effects [59]. The optimized structures are performed with the molecules in a gas phase and for the molecules surrounded by tetrahydrofuran (THF). Selected structures were re-optimized by using B3LYP [60,61] with 6−31 G(d,p) [62,63] methods, and harmonic vibrational frequency analyses were performed at the same level. B3LYP is a gradient-corrected hybrid DFT with exchange-correlation functional in Becke's three-parameter form, which includes a mixture of Hartree-Fock (HF) exchange with the Vosko, Wilk, and Nusair (VWN) functional III for local correlation and the correlation functional of Lee, Yang, and Parr (LYP) for non-local correlation. The difference is that B3LYP/LANL2DZ pseudopotential [64–66] is employed for the fully optimized of the nanocomposites/TiO2 complex system. This is the standard basis set used in the literature for modeling this type of big system, as it provides a reasonable compromise between speed and accuracy. The adsorption energy (E ad) of nanocomposites on the (TiO2)15 cluster electrode is obtained by using the following expression:
(1)
E ad
=
E
c o m p l e x
−
E
n a n o c o m p o s i t e s
−
E
T i
O 2
where
E
c o m p l e x
is the total energy of the nanocomposites/TiO2 complex system, and
E
n a n o c o m p o s i t e s
and
E
T i
O 2
are the energies of the nanocomposites and (TiO2)15 cluster, respectively. The negative value of E ad indicates that nanocomposites have been adsorbed on the TiO2 cluster.

Based on the optimized geometrical structures, the absorption spectra, vertical excitation energies, oscillator strengths, and light-harvesting efficiency (LHE) is calculated by employing the TDDFT. The B3LYP exchange-correlation potential with a 6−31 G(d,p) split valence basis set is used to description of the charge transfer and electronic properties at the ground and excited state. There are 40 states are calculated of nanocomposites and complex system in the TDDFT calculations with the polarizable continuum model [59] of THF. The relative energy of the HOMO and LUMO levels and charge separation of nanocomposites and complex systems are helpful to understand the electron transfer, free energy change of electron injection, and photosensitizer regeneration between anthoxanthin dyes, electrolyte, and TiO2 electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometrical structures and vibrational spectra of the nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The optimized geometrical nanocomposites of anthoxanthin dyes hybrid GQD and nanocomposites/TiO2 complex systems are shown in Fig. 2. To evaluate the energy and chemical stability of these nanocomposites and nanocomposites/TiO2 complex system, the harmonic frequencies, and the energy gap (Eg ) between HOMO and LUMO are calculated and collected in Table 1 . The harmonic frequencies of all calculated structures are positive, which means that all structures are stable in energy. In Table 1, the present Eg of GQD is 2.823 eV at the B3LYP/6−31 G(d,p) theoretical level, which is larger than the 1.96 eV with common DFT but smaller than the 4.68 eV with GW method [55]. The Eg s of anthoxanthin dyes from 3.697 eV to 4.888 eV, which are almost the same as those of Megala et al. [13] at B3LYP/6−31 G(d,p). These results can also confirm the reliability of the extant theoretical level. The values of Eg s for nanocomposites are from 2.740 to 2.769 eV, which means that the nanocomposites are moderate chemical stability. The Eg s are also smaller than that of the separate anthoxanthin dyes. In fact, the Eg s of the nanocomposites are the same as that of GQD, which may cause redshift of the absorption of the nanocomposites. Obviously, GQD exerts a dominant effect on the electronic properties of the nanocomposites. Moreover, the HOMO, LUMO, Eg s of nanocomposites/TiO2 complex systems are affected by the interaction between the nanocomposites and the TiO2 electrode. The obvious increase of the LUMO value and the decrease of Eg impact on the electron injection efficiency of DSSC. To visually demonstrate the vibrational spectra of the nanocomposites, nanocomposites/TiO2 complex system we have plotted all the vibrational spectra in Fig. 3 and 4
. The no imaginary vibrational frequencies of all the structures imply these nanocomposites are stable in energy. Besides, the intensity of GQD-A-TiO2 and GQD-B-TiO2 are higher than other nanocomposites in IR. Thus, GQD-B and GQD-A nanocomposites can be better candidates to harvest solar energy in the near-infrared region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the nanocomposites as photosensitizers of DSSC
ce:section.ce:para: The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the photosensitizer can provide valuable information for determining whether nanocomposites are suitable as a photosensitizer in DSSC. As photosensitizers of DSSC, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the nanocomposites must match the requirements of the band edges of the electrode and the energy level of the electrolyte to transfer the electrons from the electrolyte to the LUMO of photosensitizer and the electrode. Using the TiO2 as the electrode and the pair I−/I3 −as an electrolyte, we examine the feasibility of the present nanocomposites as the photosensitizer. Normally, it is equally desirable that the LUMO of the photosensitizer locates above the conduction band minimum (CBM) of the TiO2 electrode (-4.3 eV) [67], which can ensure the rapid injection of the electron from the photosensitizer to the electrode. However, the HOMO should be below the I−-/I3-3 − redox couple (around -4.80 eV) [68,69], which realizes the efficient regeneration of the photosensitizer. To evaluate the ability of electron injection of the excited nanocomposites to the CBM of the TiO2 electrode and regeneration of the oxidized nanocomposites by I−/I3 −redox couple, we demonstrate that the energy levels of the HOMO and LUMO for all the considered nanocomposites, the CBM energy of TiO2 electrode and the redox potential of electrolyte couple of I− /I3 − in Fig. 5 . The results show that the LUMO levels of all the considered nanocomposites are higher than the CBM of the TiO2 electrode, which makes the electron transfer from the LUMO of nanocomposites to the CBM of the TiO2 electrode. The HOMO levels of all nanocomposites are lower than the redox energy level of the I−/I3 − electrolyte couple, allowing the transfer of the electron to the ground state as a regeneration. For instance, the LUMO levels of GQD-A (-2.37 eV) is higher than the -4.30 eV of CBM for the TiO2 electrode, which indicates that the electrons can be injected into the electrode effectively. Besides, the HOMO energy level of GQD-A (-5.12 eV) is more negative than the -4.80 eV of the redox energy level of I−/I3 − electrolyte. Similarly, the interaction between nanocomposites and TiO2 electrodes has a significant effect on the energy level of the nanocomposites. The HOMO and LUMO of the nanocomposites/TiO2 complex system, the CBM of the TiO2 electrode, and the redox potential of the electrolyte couple of I−/I3 −are demonstrated in Fig. 6 . The results show that the LUMOs of the separate GQD-A-TiO2 and the GQD-B-TiO2 complex system is higher than the CBM of the TiO2 electrode, the HOMO levels are lower than the redox energy level of I−/I3 − electrolyte couple. The coupling effect between nanocomposites and the TiO2 electrode makes the HOMO, LUMO and the Eg of nanocomposites decrease, as shown in Table 1. The lower Eg enhances the absorption in the visible range of solar energy, which may increase the photocurrent in DSSC [70,71]. In this sense, the lower Eg of GQD-A-TiO2 and GQD-B-TiO2 complex systems are more suitable as photosensitizer than GQD-A and GQD-B nanocomposites.

Besides the electron transfer from the electrolyte to the nanocomposites as an important factor significantly impacting the efficiency of DSSC, the spatial separation of the charge carriers on the nanocomposites is also one for the efficiency. The effective charge spatial separation can lower the electron-hole recombination rate. Therefore, the obvious spatial charge separation, i.e., the low chance of recombination of charge carriers, is the key factor for the nanocomposites as photosensitizers of DSSC. Depending on the molecular orbital theory, the electron excitation process mainly occurs in the HOMO and the LUMO. In order to form a good charge separation state and effective electron injection, it is necessary to distribute most of the electrons on the HOMO of photosensitizer on the donor group, while the electrons on the LUMO are distributed on the receptor group. To demonstrate the spatial charge separation of the nanocomposites, we plot their HOMO and LUMO to demonstrate the contributor and acceptor of the absorption transitions in Fig. 7 . The figure indicates that the HOMOs of all nanocomposites are localized on GQD, which implicates that the GQD is the contributor of the electron for the optical absorption. Meanwhile, the LUMOs are also localized at GQD as the acceptors. As a result, the spatial charge separation of GQD is small, and the recombination of charge carriers will be fast, leading to low photovoltaic efficiency of DSSC. The charge distributions demonstrate that the LUMO of the alone anthoxanthin dye corresponds to LUMO + 2 while HOMO to HOMO-2 of nanocomposites, which leads to the energy gaps of the anthoxanthin dyes decrease in comparison to that in the alone condition. Therefore, GQD plays a role in the electronic properties of anthoxanthin dyes in the hybrid process. To further examine the charge transfer process between the nanocomposites and the semiconductor TiO2 electrode surfaces upon photoexcitation. Therefore, frontier molecular orbitals for the complex system of nanocomposites on the TiO2 electrode surface are calculated and depicted in Fig. 7, which is used to represent the nanocomposites/TiO2 interface. The upper panel shows that the HOMO is localized on the nanocomposites of the GQD-A-TiO2 complex system, and LUMO is localized on the TiO2 electrode. Thus, the obvious spatial charge separation in the optical absorption is identified for the nanocomposites. When electrons in the transition from HOMO to LUMO, it will be very beneficial to the separation of electrons and the injection of electrons from excited dyes into TiO2 semiconductors. As a contrast, in the GQD-C-TiO2 complex system, the LUMO localized on the GQD-C nanocomposites results in that the electron transfers from the TiO2 electrode but not from GQD-C to the TiO2 electrode. It is opposite the movement of an electron in standard DSSC, so GQD-C nanocomposites cannot be used as a photosensitizer. Hence, we can conclude that all nanocomposites excluding GQD-C demonstrate suitable spatial charge separation as a photosensitizer. The coupling effect between nanocomposites and the TiO2 electrode makes the complex system appear more obvious space charge separation, which is a benefit to the transfer of the photogenerated electron. Fig. 7 demonstrates that the TiO2 electrode can markedly affect the properties of the dyes photosensitizer, which also shows that the coupling effect between photosensitizer and the TiO2 electrode cannot be ignored. The HOMOs and LUMOs of GQD-A-TiO2 and GQD-B-TiO2 complex systems satisfy the requirements of the typical DSSC with the electrode of TiO2 and I− /I3 − electrolyte and demonstrate obvious spatial charge separation, resulting in the recombination of their electron and hole slowly are suitable for the photosensitizer of DSSC.

Apart from the desired band energy of electrode, HOMO/LUMO, charge spatial separation, optical properties of the nanocomposites are also vital for achieving an efficient photosensitizer of DSSC. The strong optical absorption in the visible light range is necessary to excite the electrons. To obtain the strength of the absorption of the considered structures, we calculated the UV–vis absorption spectra of all nanocomposites and nanocomposites/TiO2 complex systems of PCM-TD-B3LYP/6−31 G(d,p) level. The calculated oscillator strengths of each nanocomposite, complex system, and separate anthoxanthin dyes are presented in Fig. 8 , while the corresponding data in Table 2 . The table shows all of the nanocomposites have larger oscillator strengths and a stronger absorption wavelength range than anthoxanthin dyes. These nanocomposites have larger oscillator strengths and show stronger absorption in the wavelength range of 449.05∼456.42 nm due to the GQD and anthoxanthin dyes hybrid interaction. Fig. 8 demonstrates that all nanocomposites show red-shifted absorption in comparison with those of anthoxanthin dyes, especially for the absorption peaks in the visible light regions. Among nanocomposites, GQD-B, GQD-C, GQD-D show red-shifted absorption, while the oscillator strength was significantly higher than that of anthoxanthin dyes, which confirms that the absorptions of the nanocomposites are enhanced with a constructive interference rather than a simple additive effect [80] in the visible light range. In order to further illustrate the coupling effect of optical absorption between nanocomposites and the TiO2 electrode, the absorption spectra of the nanocomposites/TiO2 complex system are plotted as shown in Fig. 8. The coupling of nanocomposites and the TiO2 electrode significantly reduced the oscillator strength of the complex systems, but the range of light absorption was significantly red-shifted. The peak values of GQD-d-TiO2, GQD-E-TiO2, GQD-F-TiO2, GQD-G-TiO2, and GQD-H-TiO2 are about 450 nm and 750 nm respectively. The results with the TiO2 electrode demonstrate that GQD-A-TiO2 and GQD-B-TiO2 peak appears at about 500 nm, the strongest absorption range in the visible region. In particular, the peak value of GQD-C-TiO2 appears at about 850 nm in the infrared region, which does not meet the requirements of the DSSC photosensitizer. Therefore, the complex systems of GQD-A-TiO2 and GQD-B-TiO2 are more effective in obtaining solar energy than other structures, and are more suitable for the DSSC photosensitizer.

According to previous investigations [72–74], the overall conversion efficiency (η) of DSSC is calculated depends on the short-circuit current density (J sc), the open-circuit photovoltage (V oc), the fill factor (FF), and the intensity of radiance (P in):
(2)
η = F F
V oc
J sc
P in

The open-circuit photovoltage, V oc, can be calculated based on electron injection from the LUMO level of photosensitizer to the CBM of TiO2 electrode (E CBM) [75]:
(3)
V oc
=
E LUMO
−
E CBM
e

A high J sc value is associated with an intense light absorption capability and high electron injection efficiency (Φ inject) from the excited state photosensitizer to the E CBM of the TiO2 electrode. J sc as expressed by the following equation [76]:
(4)
J SC
= ∫ LHE
λ
Φ inject
η collect
dλ

J sc is proportional to the light-harvesting efficiency (LHE) at the maximum absorption wavelength, Φ inject is the electron injection efficiency, and η collect is the charge collection efficiency. Therefore, the designed photosensitizer with higher LHE and Φ inject inject values should possess higher J sc. The LHE of the photosensitizer should be as high as possible to maximize the photocurrent response. Based on Beer’s law, the calculated LHE [77,78] of photosensitizer can be quantified by the following equation:
(5) LHE = 1−10−ƒ
where f is the maximum oscillator strength of the photosensitizer associated with the wavelength corresponding to the absorption peak. The f and LHE for anthoxanthin dyes and nanocomposites are given in Table 2. Φ inject is related to the free energy change ΔG inject (in eV) for electron injection onto a titanium dioxide surface. The ΔG inject affects the electron injection rate and therefore the current density J sc in DSSC. According to Katoh [79], the ΔG inject can be calculated by the following equation:
(6)
Δ
G injec
=
E ox
dy
e *
−
E CBM
Where
E ox
dye *
represents the photoinduced excited states of the photosensitizer and E CBM represents the reduction potential of the TiO2 electrode conduction band. The
E ox
dye *
can be calculated by the following equation:
(7)
E ox
dy
e *
=
E ox
−
E
0 − 0
where E ox is the redox potential of the photosensitizer in the ground state and E 0–0 is the vertical transition energy. The free energy change
Δ
G dye regen
of photosensitizer can be calculated by the equation:
(8)
Δ
G
d y e
r e g e n
=
E
o x
−
E
r e d o x
e l e c t r o l y t e
Where
E
r e d o x
e l e c t r o l y t e
is the redox potential of iodide/tri-iodide (I−/I3 −).

The computed
E ox
dye *
, ΔG injec,
Δ
G dye regen
, LHE, and V oc of all nanocomposites are presented in Table 3 . It can be seen from the table that the ΔG injec of all nanocomposites are negative, indicating that the excited state is located above the CBM of the TiO2 electrode. The nanocomposites will spontaneously inject their excited electrons to the TiO2 electrode conduction band. The larger the value of ΔG injec, the faster will be achieved the charge transferring between the conduction band of the nanocomposites and the electrolyte. In addition,
Δ
G dye regen
of the nanocomposites are positive indicating that the redox levels of iodide/triiodide (I−/I3 −) electrolyte is higher than the ground state of nanocomposites, which can inhibit the electronic recombination. The larger the value of ΔG injec and the smaller the value of
Δ
G dye regen
, the easier the charge transfer between nanocomposites and electrolytes. The LHE values of all nanocomposites are higher than those of anthoxanthin dyes. Additionally, the V oc values of all nanocomposites are slightly lower than those of anthoxanthin dyes. The larger LHE and suitable V oc make the nanocomposites be a better performance as photosensitizers of DSSC than anthoxanthin dyes. On the whole, GQD-C nanocomposites with the higher ΔG injec, the larger LHE, appropriate V oc, the smaller
Δ
G dye regen
and the lowest
E ox
dye *
, so it has a higher driving force of injection electron. The electron injection efficiency values of the remaining seven nanocomposites are slightly lower than GQD-C. On the other hand, electron transfer of the GQD-C-TiO2 complex system indicates that it cannot be used as a photosensitizer of DSSC. Considering comprehensively, only GQD-A and GQD-B nanocomposites are identified as the most promising candidate of photosensitizer in DSSC among all the nanocomposites.

The performance of the GQD hybrid anthoxanthin dyes as the photosensitizer of DSSC is examined by using DFT/TD-DFT simulations. The photoelectric properties and intramolecular electron transfer properties of nanocomposites were studied through the geometrical structure, molecular orbital, electronic excitation and absorption spectra, light-harvesting efficiency, and intramolecular electron injection efficiency of nanocomposites. The effect of the coupling between nanocomposites and the TiO2 electrode significantly impacts the energy levels of HOMO and LUMO, the space charge separation, and the light absorption of the GQD-anthoxanthin nanocomposites. Only the GQD-A and GQD-B nanocomposites satisfy the requirements of the typical DSSC with the electrode of TiO2 and I−-/I3-3 − electrolyte. The TDDFT results show that the optical absorption ranges of the GQD-A and GQD-B nanocomposites broaden in comparison with those of the separate anthoxanthin dyes, which can improve the light capture efficiency and enhance the performance of intramolecular charge transfer. Importantly, the absorption peaks of GQD-A and GQD-B are red-shifted to the visible region after coupling with the TiO2 electrode. The GQD-B and GQD-A nanocomposites should be the most promising candidate of photosensitizer for DSSC among all the considered nanocomposites after comprehensively checking the calculational physical quantities. These findings are helpful to provide theoretical guidance for the widely screening and experimental synthesis of the photosensitizer with exceptional performance for DSSC.
Feng Gao: Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Chuan-Lu Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Gang Jiang: Data curation, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Grant No. 11574125 and No. NSFC-11874192, as well as the Taishan scholar project of Shandong province (ts201511055).
